articles.title: This Week on The Analog Antiquarian
articles.title_display: The Analog Antiquarian Pauses for Two Weeks

articles.title: Google Cloud Fraud Defence is just WEI repackaged
articles.title_display: Google Cloud Fraud Defense Repackages WEI-Style Attestation
articles.summary: Google Cloud Fraud Defense is presented as a reCAPTCHA evolution, but the article argues it is effectively Web Environment Integrity revived as a commercial product. Its core requirement is device attestation via Google Play Services / Play Integrity on modern Android or iPhone/iPad hardware, which excludes privacy-focused Android variants like GrapheneOS and LineageOS, and Firefox for Android. The author also argues the QR-code flow is easy for bot operators to bypass with cheap hardware and may train users into scanning malicious QR codes. More importantly, each successful challenge can create a persistent hardware-linked signal, turning fraud checks into a tracking mechanism across sites. The practical takeaway is that the product is framed as anti-bot defense, but its real impact is broader control and attribution over web access.

articles.title: Serving a Website on a Raspberry Pi Zero Running in RAM
articles.title_display: Serving a Website on a Pi Zero Entirely in RAM
articles.summary: This post walks through hosting a static website on a Raspberry Pi Zero v1.3 using Alpine Linux in diskless mode, with the root filesystem mounted from tmpfs/ramfs so the site runs entirely from RAM. The setup uses a 512MB Pi Zero, `lbu` to persist configuration on the microSD card, `darkhttpd` or `nginx` for web serving, and `rsync` for deployment. TLS is offloaded to a small VPS running HAProxy, with `socat` forwarding traffic from the VPS to the home network. The author also shows backup creation via a byte-for-byte `dd` image over SSH. Practical value is mainly for engineers interested in ultra-light self-hosting, diskless Linux, and low-resource deployment patterns.

articles.title: A web page that shows you everything the browser told it without asking
articles.title_display: Browser Fingerprinting Exposed as Interactive Web Story
articles.summary:
This interactive page demonstrates, in a concrete and readable way, how much a browser reveals on first load: IP-based geolocation, timezone, screen size, pixel ratio, language, GPU, installed fonts, battery status, storage quota, motion/orientation signals, referrer, and tab visibility changes. The source code shows the exact collection logic, including standard APIs such as WebGL debug renderer info, Battery API, Storage API, Visibility API, and font-width probing. It also states that only two anonymous beacons are sent to the site’s own server and that no cookies, localStorage, sessionStorage, IndexedDB, or service worker cache are used.

The technical value is mostly explanatory rather than novel research: it is a polished, source-visible demonstration of browser fingerprinting and passive tracking surfaces, with a strong emphasis on privacy implications. The page also includes practical implementation details like masking IPs in display, deterministic fingerprint-derived barcodes, and handling reduced-motion preferences. For engineers, the main takeaway is that modern browsers expose enough stable signals to identify users without cookies, and that many of these signals require no permission prompt. This makes the piece useful as a privacy awareness reference and as a compact catalog of browser-side tracking surfaces.

articles.title: Developing more confidence when tracking renames via Read­Directory­ChangesW
articles.title_display: Tracking renames more reliably with ReadDirectoryChangesExW
articles.summary: Switching from ReadDirectoryChangesW to ReadDirectoryChangesExW with ReadDirectoryNotifyExtendedInformation lets you inspect FILE_NOTIFY_EXTENDED_INFORMATION, which includes a FileId for each change. That FileId can be used to match FILE_ACTION_RENAMED_OLD_FILE and FILE_ACTION_RENAMED_NEW_FILE entries and verify they belong to the same rename operation. The post is a practical Windows filesystem tip, but it is a short implementation note rather than a deep investigation or benchmark-driven analysis.

articles.title: An Introduction to Meshtastic
articles.title_display: Meshtastic Introduces Off-Grid LoRa Mesh Messaging
articles.summary: Meshtastic’s introduction page describes an open-source, community-run system that turns inexpensive LoRa radios into an off-grid mesh messaging platform. The docs emphasize long range, no phone requirement for mesh communication, decentralized operation without a dedicated router, encrypted messaging, strong battery life, and optional GPS location features. It also notes a 331 km ground record and shows device connectivity across radios, Bluetooth, Wi‑Fi, USB, and the meshtastic-cli. For engineers, the practical value is a lightweight, license-friendly way to prototype resilient communications for remote or infrastructure-limited environments.

articles.title: A Runnable Reference Architecture for Battery Energy Storage Systems on InfluxDB 3
articles.title_display: InfluxDB 3 BESS Reference Architecture Goes Open Source
articles.summary: InfluxData published an open-source, runnable battery energy storage system (BESS) reference architecture for InfluxDB 3 Enterprise that can be started locally with Docker Compose in about two minutes. The stack is Python-first and includes a simulator generating roughly 2,000 points per second, a FastAPI + HTMX dashboard, scenarios for thermal runaway and cell drift, and database-native plugins for alerts and diagnostics. The article highlights three InfluxDB 3 features used in the design: Last Value Cache for sub-millisecond “current state” heatmaps over 768 cells, Distinct Value Cache for inventory and heartbeat queries, and the Processing Engine for Python triggers running inside the database. It also outlines how real BMS, PCS, and SCADA feeds would enter through Telegraf or OPC UA, with edge ingestion and replication to a central cluster. The practical value is a concrete, runnable blueprint for time-sensitive industrial telemetry systems, especially where high-cardinality sensor data and real-time safety logic matter.

articles.title: Mojo 1.0 Beta
articles.title_display: Mojo 1.0 Beta Launches With Python-Style Syntax
articles.summary: Mojo 1.0 beta is now available as 1.0.0b1, positioning the language as a Python-like, compiled, statically typed option for high-performance CPU and GPU programming. The announcement highlights Python interoperability, SIMD/vectorized kernel examples, compile-time metaprogramming, and a roadmap that still keeps core systems features and fuller Python compatibility in progress. The page also notes that the Mojo standard library is open source, while the compiler is planned to open-source in 2026. This is most relevant for engineers evaluating performance-oriented Python extensions or experimenting with GPU kernels in a single language, but it remains a product/positioning release rather than a deep technical report.

articles.title: Seth Michael Larson: Using Epilogue Retrace app with iPhone 13 Pro and Ubuntu
articles.title_display: Epilogue Retrace Fails on iPhone 13 Pro and Ubuntu
articles.summary: Seth Michael Larson reports that Epilogue’s Retrace app runs into two practical blockers: updating GB Operator firmware to v10.0.10 did not work from Linux Playback, and the firmware update ultimately required a Windows machine. He also found that a Lightning-to-USB-C adapter on an iPhone 13 Pro would not power the GB Operator, while a USB-C iPhone 15 Pro worked normally. The post is a useful compatibility note for anyone trying to archive Game Boy ROMs from Linux to iPhone with Epilogue hardware.

articles.title: Wander Console 0.6.0
articles.title_display: Wander Console 0.6.0 Removes URL Referral Parameters
articles.summary: Wander Console 0.6.0 removes support for the `via` referral query parameter after finding that some websites refuse to load when arbitrary query strings are appended. The author says the feature, added in 0.4.0 for access-log attribution, proved to be a misfeature even though it could be disabled in config. This release also includes minor UI adjustments and fixes, plus a changelog entry and commit reference (`b26d77c`) with more details. The practical takeaway is a small but useful design correction for a self-hosted, decentralized web console: favor compatibility over tracking convenience.
